findings.description: The HTTP response does not include the content-security-policy header.
findings.owasp: A05:2025 Security Misconfiguration / API8:2023 Security Misconfiguration
findings.evidence: Headers seen: cache-control, connection, content-length, content-type, date, etag, vary
findings.impact: Browsers cannot apply the corresponding mitigation if the header is absent.
findings.remediation: Configure the missing header at the reverse proxy or web framework layer.

findings.impact: Browsers cannot apply the corresponding mitigation if the header is absent.
findings.remediation: Configure the missing header at the reverse proxy or web framework layer.

findings.impact: Browsers cannot apply the corresponding mitigation if the header is absent.

findings.type: Public API route catalog exposes endpoint map
findings.description: The server returned a structured JSON route catalog without authentication. Listing admin, checkout, or account endpoints simplifies access-control attacks.
findings.owasp: API9:2023 Improper Inventory Management / A01:2025 Broken Access Control
findings.evidence: top-level JSON keys: service, version, docs, endpoints
reproduction: Send GET http://172.23.112.1:8080/api with no cookies or Authorization header.
Review the JSON for route/endpoint listings.
findings.impact: Attackers map the full API — including sensitive operations — before any blind guessing.
findings.remediation: Remove or authenticate machine-readable API indexes in production.

findings.description: robots.txt is reachable and lists Disallow paths worth reviewing during assessment.
findings.evidence: Disallow: /admin, Disallow: /db.config.bak, Disallow: /api/admin, Disallow: /api/coupons/apply, Disallow: /api/orders/*/receipt, Disallow: /api/account/settings
findings.impact: robots.txt is informational only, but its content can highlight non-public paths.
findings.remediation: Do not place sensitive paths in robots.txt; require authorisation server-side instead.

findings.remediation: Maintain an API inventory and remove or protect unused endpoints.

findings.type: CSP: Failure to Define Directive with No Fallback
findings.description: The Content Security Policy fails to define one of the directives that has no fallback. Missing/excluding them is the same as allowing anything. | CWE-693 | Solution: Ensure that your web server, application server, load balancer, etc. is properly configured to set the Content-Security-Policy header. | Evidence: default-src 'none'

findings.type: CSP: Failure to Define Directive with No Fallback
findings.description: The Content Security Policy fails to define one of the directives that has no fallback. Missing/excluding them is the same as allowing anything. | CWE-693 | Solution: Ensure that your web server, application server, load balancer, etc. is properly configured to set the Content-Security-Policy header. | Evidence: default-src 'none'

findings.type: Content Security Policy (CSP) Header Not Set
findings.description: Content Security Policy (CSP) is an added layer of security that helps to detect and mitigate certain types of attacks, including Cross Site Scripting (XSS) and data injection attacks. These attacks are used for everything from data theft to site defacement or distribution of malware. CSP provides a set of standard HTTP headers that allow website owners to declare approved sources of content that browsers should be allowed to load on that page — covered types are JavaScript, CSS, HTML frames, fonts, images and embeddable objects such as Java applets, ActiveX, audio and video files. | CWE-693 | Solution: Ensure that your web server, application server, load balancer, etc. is configured to set the Content-Security-Policy header.

The response does not protect against 'ClickJacking' attacks. It should include either Content-Security-Policy with 'frame-ancestors' directive or X-Frame-Options. | CWE-1021 | Solution: Modern Web browsers support the Content-Security-Policy and X-Frame-Options HTTP headers. Ensure one of them is set on all web pages returned by your site/app.If you expect the page to be framed only by pages on your server (e.g. it's part of a FRAMESET) then you'll want to use SAMEORIGIN, otherwise if you never expect the page to be framed, you should use DENY. Alternatively consider implementing Content Security Policy's "frame-ancestors" directive.

The response does not protect against 'ClickJacking' attacks. It should include either Content-Security-Policy with 'frame-ancestors' directive or X-Frame-Options. | CWE-1021 | Solution: Modern Web browsers support the Content-Security-Policy and X-Frame-Options HTTP headers. Ensure one of them is set on all web pages returned by your site/app.If you expect the page to be framed only by pages on your server (e.g. it's part of a FRAMESET) then you'll want to use SAMEORIGIN, otherwise if you never expect the page to be framed, you should use DENY. Alternatively consider implementing Content Security Policy's "frame-ancestors" directive.

Ensure one of them is set on all web pages returned by your site/app.If you expect the page to be framed only by pages on your server (e.g. it's part of a FRAMESET) then you'll want to use SAMEORIGIN, otherwise if you never expect the page to be framed, you should use DENY. Alternatively consider implementing Content Security Policy's "frame-ancestors" directive.

Cross-Origin-Embedder-Policy header is a response header that prevents a document from loading any cross-origin resources that don't explicitly grant the document permission (using CORP or CORS). | CWE-693 | Solution: Ensure that the application/web server sets the Cross-Origin-Embedder-Policy header appropriately, and that it sets the Cross-Origin-Embedder-Policy header to 'require-corp' for documents.If possible, ensure that the end user uses a standards-compliant and modern web browser that supports the Cross-Origin-Embedder-Policy header (https://caniuse.com/mdn-http_headers_cross-origin-embedder-policy).

Cross-Origin-Embedder-Policy header is a response header that prevents a document from loading any cross-origin resources that don't explicitly grant the document permission (using CORP or CORS). | CWE-693 | Solution: Ensure that the application/web server sets the Cross-Origin-Embedder-Policy header appropriately, and that it sets the Cross-Origin-Embedder-Policy header to 'require-corp' for documents.If possible, ensure that the end user uses a standards-compliant and modern web browser that supports the Cross-Origin-Embedder-Policy header (https://caniuse.com/mdn-http_headers_cross-origin-embedder-policy).

Cross-Origin-Embedder-Policy header is a response header that prevents a document from loading any cross-origin resources that don't explicitly grant the document permission (using CORP or CORS). | CWE-693 | Solution: Ensure that the application/web server sets the Cross-Origin-Embedder-Policy header appropriately, and that it sets the Cross-Origin-Embedder-Policy header to 'require-corp' for documents.If possible, ensure that the end user uses a standards-compliant and modern web browser that supports the Cross-Origin-Embedder-Policy header (https://caniuse.com/mdn-http_headers_cross-origin-embedder-policy).

Cross-Origin-Opener-Policy header is a response header that allows a site to control if others included documents share the same browsing context. Sharing the same browsing context with untrusted documents might lead to data leak. | CWE-693 | Solution: Ensure that the application/web server sets the Cross-Origin-Opener-Policy header appropriately, and that it sets the Cross-Origin-Opener-Policy header to 'same-origin' for documents.'same-origin-allow-popups' is considered as less secured and should be avoided.If possible, ensure that the end user uses a standards-compliant and modern web browser that supports the Cross-Origin-Opener-Policy header (https://caniuse.com/mdn-http_headers_cross-origin-opener-policy).

Cross-Origin-Opener-Policy header is a response header that allows a site to control if others included documents share the same browsing context. Sharing the same browsing context with untrusted documents might lead to data leak. | CWE-693 | Solution: Ensure that the application/web server sets the Cross-Origin-Opener-Policy header appropriately, and that it sets the Cross-Origin-Opener-Policy header to 'same-origin' for documents.'same-origin-allow-popups' is considered as less secured and should be avoided.If possible, ensure that the end user uses a standards-compliant and modern web browser that supports the Cross-Origin-Opener-Policy header (https://caniuse.com/mdn-http_headers_cross-origin-opener-policy).

Cross-Origin-Opener-Policy header is a response header that allows a site to control if others included documents share the same browsing context. Sharing the same browsing context with untrusted documents might lead to data leak. | CWE-693 | Solution: Ensure that the application/web server sets the Cross-Origin-Opener-Policy header appropriately, and that it sets the Cross-Origin-Opener-Policy header to 'same-origin' for documents.'same-origin-allow-popups' is considered as less secured and should be avoided.If possible, ensure that the end user uses a standards-compliant and modern web browser that supports the Cross-Origin-Opener-Policy header (https://caniuse.com/mdn-http_headers_cross-origin-opener-policy).

Cross-Origin-Resource-Policy header is an opt-in header designed to counter side-channels attacks like Spectre. Resource should be specifically set as shareable amongst different origins. | CWE-693 | Solution: Ensure that the application/web server sets the Cross-Origin-Resource-Policy header appropriately, and that it sets the Cross-Origin-Resource-Policy header to 'same-origin' for all web pages.'same-site' is considered as less secured and should be avoided.If resources must be shared, set the header to 'cross-origin'.If possible, ensure that the end user uses a standards-compliant and modern web browser that supports the Cross-Origin-Resource-Policy header (https://caniuse.com/mdn-http_headers_cross-origin-resource-policy).

Cross-Origin-Resource-Policy header is an opt-in header designed to counter side-channels attacks like Spectre. Resource should be specifically set as shareable amongst different origins. | CWE-693 | Solution: Ensure that the application/web server sets the Cross-Origin-Resource-Policy header appropriately, and that it sets the Cross-Origin-Resource-Policy header to 'same-origin' for all web pages.'same-site' is considered as less secured and should be avoided.If resources must be shared, set the header to 'cross-origin'.If possible, ensure that the end user uses a standards-compliant and modern web browser that supports the Cross-Origin-Resource-Policy header (https://caniuse.com/mdn-http_headers_cross-origin-resource-policy).

Cross-Origin-Resource-Policy header is an opt-in header designed to counter side-channels attacks like Spectre. Resource should be specifically set as shareable amongst different origins. | CWE-693 | Solution: Ensure that the application/web server sets the Cross-Origin-Resource-Policy header appropriately, and that it sets the Cross-Origin-Resource-Policy header to 'same-origin' for all web pages.'same-site' is considered as less secured and should be avoided.If resources must be shared, set the header to 'cross-origin'.If possible, ensure that the end user uses a standards-compliant and modern web browser that supports the Cross-Origin-Resource-Policy header (https://caniuse.com/mdn-http_headers_cross-origin-resource-policy).

Cross-Origin-Resource-Policy header is an opt-in header designed to counter side-channels attacks like Spectre. Resource should be specifically set as shareable amongst different origins. | CWE-693 | Solution: Ensure that the application/web server sets the Cross-Origin-Resource-Policy header appropriately, and that it sets the Cross-Origin-Resource-Policy header to 'same-origin' for all web pages.'same-site' is considered as less secured and should be avoided.If resources must be shared, set the header to 'cross-origin'.If possible, ensure that the end user uses a standards-compliant and modern web browser that supports the Cross-Origin-Resource-Policy header (https://caniuse.com/mdn-http_headers_cross-origin-resource-policy).

Cross-Origin-Resource-Policy header is an opt-in header designed to counter side-channels attacks like Spectre. Resource should be specifically set as shareable amongst different origins. | CWE-693 | Solution: Ensure that the application/web server sets the Cross-Origin-Resource-Policy header appropriately, and that it sets the Cross-Origin-Resource-Policy header to 'same-origin' for all web pages.'same-site' is considered as less secured and should be avoided.If resources must be shared, set the header to 'cross-origin'.If possible, ensure that the end user uses a standards-compliant and modern web browser that supports the Cross-Origin-Resource-Policy header (https://caniuse.com/mdn-http_headers_cross-origin-resource-policy).

Permissions Policy Header is an added layer of security that helps to restrict from unauthorized access or usage of browser/client features by web resources. This policy ensures the user privacy by limiting or specifying the features of the browsers can be used by the web resources. Permissions Policy provides a set of standard HTTP headers that allow website owners to limit which features of browsers can be used by the page such as camera, microphone, location, full screen etc. | CWE-693 | Solution: Ensure that your web server, application server, load balancer, etc. is configured to set the Permissions-Policy header.

The Anti-MIME-Sniffing header X-Content-Type-Options was not set to 'nosniff'. This allows older versions of Internet Explorer and Chrome to perform MIME-sniffing on the response body, potentially causing the response body to be interpreted and displayed as a content type other than the declared content type. Current (early 2014) and legacy versions of Firefox will use the declared content type (if one is set), rather than performing MIME-sniffing. | CWE-693 | Solution: Ensure that the application/web server sets the Content-Type header appropriately, and that it sets the X-Content-Type-Options header to 'nosniff' for all web pages.If possible, ensure that the end user uses a standards-compliant and modern web browser that does not perform MIME-sniffing at all, or that can be directed by the web application/web server to not perform MIME-sniffing.

The Anti-MIME-Sniffing header X-Content-Type-Options was not set to 'nosniff'. This allows older versions of Internet Explorer and Chrome to perform MIME-sniffing on the response body, potentially causing the response body to be interpreted and displayed as a content type other than the declared content type. Current (early 2014) and legacy versions of Firefox will use the declared content type (if one is set), rather than performing MIME-sniffing. | CWE-693 | Solution: Ensure that the application/web server sets the Content-Type header appropriately, and that it sets the X-Content-Type-Options header to 'nosniff' for all web pages.If possible, ensure that the end user uses a standards-compliant and modern web browser that does not perform MIME-sniffing at all, or that can be directed by the web application/web server to not perform MIME-sniffing.

The Content-Type header was either missing or empty. | CWE-345 | Solution: Ensure each page is setting the specific and appropriate content-type value for the content being delivered.

The response contents are not storable by caching components such as proxy servers. If the response does not contain sensitive, personal or user-specific information, it may benefit from being stored and cached, to improve performance. | CWE-524 | Solution: The content may be marked as storable by ensuring that the following conditions are satisfied:The request method must be understood by the cache and defined as being cacheable ("GET", "HEAD", and "POST" are currently defined as cacheable)The response status code must be understood by the cache (one of the 1XX, 2XX, 3XX, 4XX, or 5XX response classes are generally understood)The "no-store" cache directive must not appear in the request or response header fieldsFor caching by "shared" caches such as "proxy" caches, the "private" response directive must not appear in the responseFor caching by "shared" caches such as "proxy" caches, the "Authorization" header field must not appear in the request, unless the response explicitly allows it (using one of the "must-revalidate", "public", or "s-maxage" Cache-Control response directives)In addition to the conditions above, at least one of the following conditions must also be satisfied by the response:It must contain an "Expires" header fieldIt must contain a "max-age" response directiveFor "shared" caches such as "proxy" caches, it must contain a "s-maxage" response directiveIt must contain a "Cache Control Extension" that allows it to be cachedIt must have a status code that is defined as cacheable by default (200, 203, 204, 206, 300, 301, 404, 405, 410, 414, 501). | Evidence: 401

The response contents are not storable by caching components such as proxy servers. If the response does not contain sensitive, personal or user-specific information, it may benefit from being stored and cached, to improve performance. | CWE-524 | Solution: The content may be marked as storable by ensuring that the following conditions are satisfied:The request method must be understood by the cache and defined as being cacheable ("GET", "HEAD", and "POST" are currently defined as cacheable)The response status code must be understood by the cache (one of the 1XX, 2XX, 3XX, 4XX, or 5XX response classes are generally understood)The "no-store" cache directive must not appear in the request or response header fieldsFor caching by "shared" caches such as "proxy" caches, the "private" response directive must not appear in the responseFor caching by "shared" caches such as "proxy" caches, the "Authorization" header field must not appear in the request, unless the response explicitly allows it (using one of the "must-revalidate", "public", or "s-maxage" Cache-Control response directives)In addition to the conditions above, at least one of the following conditions must also be satisfied by the response:It must contain an "Expires" header fieldIt must contain a "max-age" response directiveFor "shared" caches such as "proxy" caches, it must contain a "s-maxage" response directiveIt must contain a "Cache Control Extension" that allows it to be cachedIt must have a status code that is defined as cacheable by default (200, 203, 204, 206, 300, 301, 404, 405, 410, 414, 501). | Evidence: 401

The response contents are storable by caching components such as proxy servers, and may be retrieved directly from the cache, rather than from the origin server by the caching servers, in response to similar requests from other users. If the response data is sensitive, personal or user-specific, this may result in sensitive information being leaked. In some cases, this may even result in a user gaining complete control of the session of another user, depending on the configuration of the caching components in use in their environment. This is primarily an issue where "shared" caching servers such as "proxy" caches are configured on the local network. This configuration is typically found in corporate or educational environments, for instance. | CWE-524 | Solution: Validate that the response does not contain sensitive, personal or user-specific information. If it does, consider the use of the following HTTP response headers, to limit, or prevent the content being stored and retrieved from the cache by another user:Cache-Control: no-cache, no-store, must-revalidate, privatePragma: no-cacheExpires: 0This configuration directs both HTTP 1.0 and HTTP 1.1 compliant caching servers to not store the response, and to not retrieve the response (without validation) from the cache, in response to a similar request.

The response contents are storable by caching components such as proxy servers, and may be retrieved directly from the cache, rather than from the origin server by the caching servers, in response to similar requests from other users. If the response data is sensitive, personal or user-specific, this may result in sensitive information being leaked. In some cases, this may even result in a user gaining complete control of the session of another user, depending on the configuration of the caching components in use in their environment. This is primarily an issue where "shared" caching servers such as "proxy" caches are configured on the local network. This configuration is typically found in corporate or educational environments, for instance. | CWE-524 | Solution: Validate that the response does not contain sensitive, personal or user-specific information. If it does, consider the use of the following HTTP response headers, to limit, or prevent the content being stored and retrieved from the cache by another user:Cache-Control: no-cache, no-store, must-revalidate, privatePragma: no-cacheExpires: 0This configuration directs both HTTP 1.0 and HTTP 1.1 compliant caching servers to not store the response, and to not retrieve the response (without validation) from the cache, in response to a similar request.

The response contents are storable by caching components such as proxy servers, but will not be retrieved directly from the cache, without validating the request upstream, in response to similar requests from other users. | CWE-524 | Evidence: no-cache

A malformed parameter caused a verbose or inconsistent error page.
findings.owasp: A05:2025 Security Misconfiguration / API8:2023 Security Misconfiguration
reproduction: Observe verbose error output or inconsistent handling.
findings.impact: Verbose errors can reveal routing, framework, or defensive-control behavior.
findings.remediation: Return generic errors and log detailed diagnostics server-side only.

findings.description: A marker payload was reflected into the HTTP response.
reproduction: Search the response body for the marker payload.
findings.impact: Reflected input may become executable script if output encoding is missing.
findings.remediation: Contextually encode reflected data and validate input.

findings.description: An API endpoint returned structured data without authentication.
findings.impact: Public inventory endpoints expand the discoverable attack surface.
findings.remediation: Document intentionally public APIs and remove stale or hidden endpoints.

findings.description: An API endpoint returned structured data without authentication.
findings.owasp: API3:2023 Broken Object Property Level Authorization / A01:2025 Broken Access Control
findings.evidence: Keys: account, admin, auth, cart, challenges, coupons, docs, endpoints, health, orders, products, service, supportChat, user, users, version
findings.impact: Sensitive object properties can expose credentials, tokens, roles, emails or payment data.
findings.remediation: Filter sensitive fields and enforce object/property authorization.

findings.description: An API endpoint returned structured data without authentication.
findings.evidence: Keys: category_id, category_name, created_at, created_by, description, id, image_url, name, price, products, stock, updated_at
findings.impact: Public inventory endpoints expand the discoverable attack surface.
findings.remediation: Document intentionally public APIs and remove stale or hidden endpoints.

findings.description: An API endpoint returned structured data without authentication.
findings.evidence: Keys: category, challenges, description, difficulty, id, label, learningObjectives, name, panel, route, slug, solved, solvedAt, summary, surface, title
findings.impact: Public inventory endpoints expand the discoverable attack surface.
findings.remediation: Document intentionally public APIs and remove stale or hidden endpoints.

findings.description: An API endpoint returned structured data without authentication.
findings.impact: Public structured APIs should be reviewed and documented as intended exposure.
findings.remediation: Classify public APIs and remove accidental exposure.

findings.description: An API endpoint returned structured data without authentication.
findings.impact: Public inventory endpoints expand the discoverable attack surface.
findings.remediation: Document intentionally public APIs and remove stale or hidden endpoints.

findings.description: 15 endpoint(s) advertise state-changing HTTP methods.
findings.owasp: API5:2023 Broken Function Level Authorization / API6:2023 Unrestricted Access to Sensitive Business Flows
findings.evidence: Allowed methods: GET,HEAD,PUT,PATCH,POST,DELETE; examples: http://172.23.112.1:8080/api/auth/jwt-flag, http://172.23.112.1:8080/api/products/search?q=test, http://172.23.112.1:8080/api, http://172.23.112.1:8080/api/admin, http://172.23.112.1:8080/api/coupons/apply
findings.impact: Unsafe methods can become exploitable if authorization or CSRF controls are weak.
findings.remediation: Expose only required methods and enforce authorization on state changes.

findings.description: Repeated invalid login attempts were accepted without HTTP 429 / lockout responses.
findings.owasp: API4:2023 Unrestricted Resource Consumption / A07:2025 Identification and Authentication Failures
reproduction: Send 8 repeated POSTs with bogus credentials to http://172.23.112.1:8080/api/auth/login
findings.impact: Attackers may automate brute force, scraping, or resource consumption workflows.
findings.remediation: Add rate limits, lockouts, and abuse detection to authentication endpoints.

findings.description: A state-changing endpoint accepted a boundary/invalid value.
findings.owasp: A04:2025 Insecure Design / API6:2023 Unrestricted Access to Sensitive Business Flows
findings.impact: Weak server-side validation can enable business logic abuse.
findings.remediation: Validate value ranges server-side for every state-changing endpoint.

findings.description: An anonymously reachable index/catalog response triggered generic secret heuristics. Treat as informational until manually confirmed.
findings.owasp: A05:2025 Security Misconfiguration / API9:2023 Improper Inventory Management
reproduction: Fetch the configured catalog path(s); diff against intentional public data.
findings.impact: Pre-auth disclosure can simplify subsequent testing.
findings.remediation: Keep deployment secrets out of public metadata.

findings.type: Operational-looking strings mined from authenticated JSON
findings.description: Heuristics surfaced strings resembling codes/secrets (http://172.23.112.1:8080/api/challenges → APP02, APP05, APP06, APP07, APP08).
findings.owasp: API3:2023 Broken Object Property Level Authorization / A05:2025 Security Misconfiguration
findings.evidence: http://172.23.112.1:8080/api/challenges → APP02, APP05, APP06, APP07, APP08
reproduction: Diff JSON against the documented public schema.
findings.impact: Leakage may enable chaining by any logged-in caller.
findings.remediation: Filter fields at serialization time.

findings.description: Varying a numeric segment seen in crawler output produced HTTP 200 with token-shaped content for the harness account.
findings.owasp: A01:2025 Broken Access Control / API1:2023 Broken Object Level Authorization
reproduction: Enumerate adjacent numeric identifiers with the harness account.
findings.impact: Unauthorized object access cannot be ruled out without server-side authorization review.
findings.remediation: Authorize per resource key; deny by default.

findings.description: Optional alg=none JWT probe at api/auth/jwt-flag returned HTTP 200 with sensitive content (matrix entry {'id': 1, 'username': 'admin', 'role': 'admin', 'iat': 1777992437}). Requires --experimental-auth + operator config.
findings.owasp: A07:2025 Identification and Authentication Failures / API2:2023 Broken Authentication
reproduction: Replay payload + path triple recorded in evidence using your JWT tool of choice.
findings.impact: Trust in unsigned bearer claims breaks authentication.
findings.remediation: Reject ``alg=none`` tokens; pin verification algorithms.

findings.description: A public directory or file path exposed downloadable sensitive-looking artifacts.
findings.owasp: A05:2025 Security Misconfiguration / API3:2023 Broken Object Property Level Authorization
findings.impact: Exposed artifacts can leak source, dependencies, secrets, database backups, or internal documents.
findings.remediation: Remove backup/data files from the web root and disable directory listing.

findings.description: A crafted query parameter produced database-shaped error behavior.
reproduction: Observe database error wording or HTTP 5xx behavior.
findings.impact: The parameter may be injectable or reveal database internals useful for follow-up tooling.
findings.remediation: Use parameterized queries and generic error responses.

findings.type: JWT Algorithm None Bypass: Admin Access Confirmed
findings.description: An unsigned JWT with an elevated role claim was accepted; the endpoint returned sensitive content.
findings.owasp: A07:2025 Identification and Authentication Failures / API2:2023 Broken Authentication
reproduction: Craft a JWT with header {alg:none} and an admin role in the payload.
findings.impact: Unsigned tokens can bypass authentication and authorization checks for any caller.
findings.remediation: Reject alg=none tokens and only accept explicitly configured signed algorithms.

findings.description: Parameter `q` is vulnerable. Technique: stacked queries. Title: PostgreSQL > 8.1 stacked queries (comment).
findings.evidence: parameter=q; technique=stacked queries; title=PostgreSQL > 8.1 stacked queries (comment)
findings.remediation: Use parameterized queries and server-side allowlists for dynamic clauses.

findings.remediation: Use parameterized queries and server-side allowlists for dynamic clauses.
